params.text:
Post-incident support, reporting, and lone working

What happens after a conflict matters as much as how you handle it in the moment. Incidents and near misses should always be recorded and reported — this protects you, supports the person, and gives your organisation the information it needs to reduce future risk.

  • Support — after a difficult incident, everyone involved, including witnesses, should be offered a chance to talk and access support. Distress after an incident is normal.
  • Debrief — reflect on what triggered the situation, what worked, and what could be done differently next time.
  • Record and report — write a clear, factual, timely account and report through your organisation's channels so patterns and risks can be identified.
  • Update the care plan — note known triggers and successful approaches so colleagues can prevent a repeat.

Lone working and personal safety. Many carers work alone in the community, where help is not close at hand. Follow your lone-working policy: share your schedule, keep a charged phone and any personal alarm, carry out a dynamic risk assessment on arrival, and check in as agreed. If a situation feels unsafe, leave and raise the alarm.

params.question: Which actions should follow a conflict or aggressive incident? Select all that apply.

answers.text: Offer support to everyone involved, including any witnesses.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Yes. Support after an incident is important for all those affected, not only the person directly targeted.

answers.text: Record a clear, factual account and report it through the correct channels.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Timely, factual recording and reporting helps identify risks and prevent recurrence.

answers.text: Debrief and update the care plan with known triggers and what worked.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Yes. Reflecting and updating the plan helps colleagues prevent similar incidents in future.

answers.text: Keep the incident to yourself to avoid making a fuss or getting the person into trouble.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Not reporting hides risk and denies support. Incidents and near misses should always be recorded and reported.
